content:
Colorado wrote the toughest AI rules in the country. They were set to go live June 30. Instead the state deleted them and the reason it folded is the real story.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image by Ahmed M. AbdelfattahOn June 30, 2026, the most comprehensive artificial intelligence law in the United States was scheduled to take effect.
It will not. Not because a court struck it down. Not because the deadline slipped again. It will not take effect because the state that wrote it Colorado repealed it first.
A few weeks ago I wrote about Congress trying, for the third time, to block states from making their own AI rules. The argument then was that a patchwork of fifty different state laws would choke the industry, so Washington kept attempting to freeze state regulation from above.
Here is what has become clear since. Congress did not need to win that fight.
The states are folding on their own.
Colorado’s Retreat, Compressed Into One Statute
In 2024, Colorado passed SB 24–205, a genuinely ambitious law. It treated “high-risk” AI systems the kind used to decide who gets hired, housed, insured, or…
